region_of_origin: East Asia and coastal regions globally

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for dried seaweed as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for processed marine vegetables is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of dried seaweed is approximately 5g (one small sheet or a small handful of flakes), as it is highly concentrated and consumed in small quantities.

nutrient_source.title: Dried Seaweed — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for dried seaweed (record ID F14050002). All per-100g values are taken as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for generic dried seaweed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of approximately 17 is estimated based on the extremely low available carbohydrate content (2.3g/100g) and the high alginate and dietary fibre content (34.3g/100g), which are well-documented to blunt glycaemic response. This estimate is consistent with published GI values for low-carbohydrate, high-fibre marine vegetables. Glycemic load per 5g serving = (17 × 2.3 × 5/100) / 100 ≈ 0.2, which is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 470mg/100g offset by very high potassium 2900mg/100g; serving size is typically 2–5g making actual sodium intake clinically negligible
hypertension.guidance: At a typical 5g serving, dried seaweed contributes only ~24mg sodium and ~145mg potassium; individuals with hypertension may consume it in small culinary quantities, but should avoid large servings (>20g) and choose unsalted or low-sodium varieties.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low available carbohydrate (2.3g/100g), negligible sugar (0.3g/100g), and exceptionally high dietary fibre (34.3g/100g) with bioactive alginate and fucoxanthin supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dried seaweed is well-suited for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content, high fibre, and bioactive compounds (alginate, fucoxanthin) with evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties; choose plain unseasoned varieties to avoid added sugars and sodium.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary fibre (34.3g/100g), very high potassium (2900mg/100g), low saturated fat (~1.0g/100g), zero cholesterol, and marine omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dried seaweed provides a favourable cardiovascular nutrient profile with high fibre, potassium, and marine omega-3s at low saturated fat; consume in typical culinary portions of 2–10g and select unsalted products to minimise sodium contribution.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Marine omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA), fucoxanthin polyphenols, and high dietary fibre with low saturated fat support anti-inflammatory dietary patterns
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dried seaweed is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory omega-3 content and polyphenols; limit individual servings to 5–10g to avoid excess iodine and sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried seaweed is a minimally processed marine vegetable product made by dehydrating various species of edible algae, including nori (Pyropia yezoensis), wakame (Undaria pinnatifida), kombu (Saccharina japonica), and kelp. The result is dark green to black sheets or flakes with a crisp, papery texture and a distinctly salty, oceanic umami flavour. It is widely consumed across East Asian cuisines — particularly Japanese, Korean, and Chinese — as a snack, soup ingredient, sushi wrapper, or seasoning. Per 100g, dried seaweed is nutritionally exceptional: it provides 303 kcal, 46.7g protein, 4.3g fat, only 2.3g carbohydrate, and a remarkable 34.3g dietary fibre. It is also rich in potassium (2900mg), calcium (310mg), and iron (19mg), and contains marine omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) and bioactive compounds including alginate and fucoxanthin. Sodium is 470mg/100g, but at typical serving sizes of 2–5g, actual sodium intake is clinically negligible (9–24mg). The glycaemic index is very low (estimated ~17) due to minimal available carbohydrate and high soluble fibre. Dried seaweed is suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. It is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Iodine content is high — particularly in kelp — and excessive daily consumption should be avoided to prevent thyroid disruption.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in dried seaweed?
Is seaweed good for blood pressure?
What are the nutrients in nori?
Is dried seaweed high in protein?
Can I eat seaweed on a keto diet?
Is seaweed good for heart health?
How much fibre is in dried seaweed?

ingredient_synonyms.nori: dried laver sheets used in Japanese cuisine
ingredient_synonyms.wakame: dried brown seaweed used in miso soup and salads
ingredient_synonyms.kombu: dried kelp used for dashi stock
ingredient_synonyms.kelp: large brown seaweed, dried form used as seasoning
ingredient_synonyms.laver: traditional English term for edible seaweed
ingredient_synonyms.sea vegetable: culinary term for edible marine algae
ingredient_synonyms.marine algae: scientific category encompassing all edible seaweed types

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (record F14050002) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input. The source is an approved primary regional source. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from analogous evidence given the absence of a direct measurement, which is documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for dried seaweed is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F14050002; USDA FoodData Central does not provide a single consolidated dried seaweed entry with full fatty acid profile. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food record. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — dried seaweed is a plant-derived marine food with no industrial trans fats). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-derived food, no cholesterol). saturated_fat_g of 1.0g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous dried seaweed entries as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately. GI of 17 is an analogous estimate based on very low available carbohydrate (2.3g/100g) and very high dietary fibre (34.3g/100g); no direct GI measurement for generic dried seaweed exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dietary_tag 'wholegrain' from the input has been removed as dried seaweed is not a grain product. The dietary_tag 'keto' is retained as the food has very low net carbohydrates (2.3g/100g minus 34.3g fibre = effectively 0g net carbs). Iodine content is notably high in seaweed species, especially kelp; excessive daily consumption (>10g/day) may risk iodine toxicity and thyroid disruption — this is not captured in the standard nutrient fields but is clinically relevant.
